# Questions in Livestream 5

Live Stream #5: Five and Even More Questions.

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45</a> | Which king created the Great Sphinx: Khafre or Djedefre? | He corrects himself and says the evidence points strongly to Khafre. | He says he had misspoken previously, then points to the archaeology around the Sphinx temple, river temple, causeway, and Khafre's mortuary complex as the reason Khafre is the prevailing view. Djedefre's pyramid was away from Giza, in poor condition, and not a good fit for the Sphinx. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=309" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:09</a> | Can the Song of Moses in Exodus 15 be used as evidence because it is old, and are other Torah sections especially old? | He says many Pentateuchal source texts look very old, especially Genesis/Joseph and Exodus material, though he has not studied Exodus 15 closely enough to make a specific claim. | He says the Joseph and Exodus accounts contain late Bronze Age vocabulary, place names, itinerary details, and Egyptian cultural markers, so he thinks many Pentateuchal sources are much older than the dominant scholarly view allows. He has not done a detailed study of Exodus 15 specifically, so he treats it as worth investigating rather than making a firm claim about that chapter. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=493" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:13</a> | What are your thoughts on the Admonitions of Ipuwer? | He treats it as a Middle Kingdom prophetic text that reflects Egyptian fears later echoed by exodus plague imagery. | He places Ipuwer in a Middle Kingdom genre of ex eventu prophetic literature used to legitimate new rule by portraying the old order as chaotic. In his reading, the text is not an exodus report, but its images of social disorder, darkness, blood, flies, and Semites in the land show deep Egyptian fears that later exodus plague language could tap into. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=752" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:32</a> | Are you familiar with the work of Algis Uzdavinys, and what are your thoughts? | He was not familiar with the name and did not comment on the work. | He says he has never heard the name and would need more information about the person's work before commenting. The only substantive answer he gives is that he cannot responsibly say more because he is unfamiliar with the scholar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=814" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:34</a> | Does Joseph's Egyptian name date him, and would a seven-year famine leave scientific evidence? | Joseph's name is genuinely Egyptian but not narrowly datable; a famine would be hard to detect because Egypt often lived near subsistence. | He reads the name as having a real Egyptian etymology, especially the `pa-ankh` element, but says it appears too broadly to date Joseph the way a name like Ramesses can. He also says famines were periodic in Egypt because Nile conditions depended on eastern African rains, and chronic malnutrition even among mummified elites would make a specific seven-year famine difficult to isolate scientifically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=1001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:41</a> | Why is Egyptian presence not mentioned much in Judges, Samuel, and Kings? | Kings does mention later Egyptian campaigns; Judges and Samuel occur when Egypt was largely too internally troubled to campaign in the Levant. | He distinguishes the Hyksos issue from later Egyptian campaigns, saying the term Hyksos was an Egyptian label that had largely passed from memory by the exodus period. Kings does preserve campaigns such as Shoshenq and Taharqa, but during Judges and Samuel Egypt had lost the Levant, suffered internal instability and hyperinflation after Ramesses IV, and was not in a position to keep campaigning abroad. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=1370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:50</a> | During famine, how could Egypt support migrants, and were they farming or buying surplus food? | Egypt's delta and stored grain could support migrants; foreigners could herd flocks and trade for grain rather than receive food freely. | He says famine migrants from the Levant and Libya could settle in less densely occupied delta areas, where game, fish, fowl, and grazing were available after the Neolithic wet period ended. In the Joseph famine, he connects the food supply to taxation and stored grain, with temple or state granaries acting as emergency reserves; migrants were mainly herding flocks and trading livestock or valuables for grain rather than simply being given food. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=1670" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:50</a> | Could KV5 be connected to the death of the firstborn of Egypt? | He says yes, in that it was built for Ramesses II's children and likely began after the death of his firstborn. | He says KV5 was built for the children of Ramesses II and that an inspection report confirms the tomb of Amun-her-khepeshef, Ramesses' firstborn, was there. He thinks the tomb's unusual scale and uniqueness make it plausible that its construction began in response to the firstborn's death, though he frames the connection cautiously. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=1798" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:58</a> | How long did it usually take to build a pyramid or a temple? | It varied widely: simple temples could take a year or two, major temple complexes centuries, and pyramids from a few years to around 30 years. | He says construction time depended on size, complexity, decoration, and engineering problems. Small temples or queen's pyramids could be built in only a few years, while Karnak and Luxor temple work stretched across multiple reigns, and Khufu's Great Pyramid may have taken about 30 years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=1962" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:42</a> | Where did the Israelites get cattle for tabernacle sacrifices if God sent quail for food? | He says they had cattle, sheep, and goats from Egypt, but sacrifices were costly and could reduce their food resources. | He says Israelites at Avaris would have had cattle, sheep, goats, oxen for plowing, and animals for carts, even if Egyptian and Semitic dietary habits differed. In the wilderness those animals could not reproduce as easily, so eating or sacrificing them created real scarcity; that cost is part of what makes sacrifice meaningful, while the quail stories show divine provision alongside that economic pressure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=2259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:39</a> | Where did the Israelites get expensive tabernacle materials after leaving Egypt in a hurry? | He says most precious materials came from Egyptian neighbors as unpaid wages or compensation, while wood and some materials came from the Sinai environment. | He interprets the request for gold, silver, bronze, dyed materials, skins, and other valuables from Egyptian neighbors as compensation for forced labor, tied to group responsibility because Egyptian society benefited from Israelite labor. Acacia wood and some other materials could come from the Sinai environment, and the tabernacle collection later pooled these resources from among the Israelites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=2505" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:45</a> | Do you know anything about the 10,000-year-old basket found in Israel? | He knows little beyond that it appears to be a Neolithic woven basket and awaits more publication. | He says he has only heard a little about the find: it appears to be a woven Neolithic basket, not a chest, and it was recovered in an excavation context. He avoids going beyond that because more details had not yet been published. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=2578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:58</a> | How do you respond to scholars who say Israelites were originally polytheistic and monotheism came later? | He largely agrees Israel struggled with polytheism, but places the beginning of monotheism at Sinai and its full lesson after the exile. | He says the archaeological and biblical evidence both indicate Israel was polytheistic in Egypt, with little clear evidence for Yahweh worship and evidence for deities such as Baal, Resheph, and Astarte at Avaris. He sees Sinai as the first real beginning of Israelite monotheism, but says Israel continued returning to idolatry until the exile finally drove the covenant lesson home. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=2878" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:58</a> | Is there a connection between Apophis and Leviathan or Lotan? | He does not see a strong connection and treats Apophis and Leviathan as distinct mythological figures. | He says Apophis appears relatively late in Egyptian myth as a solar-myth serpent or demon opposing the sun boat, while Leviathan seems more connected with primordial waters or other Near Eastern serpent traditions. Because their settings and mythological roles differ, he does not see a meaningful direct connection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=3017" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:17</a> | Is Nimrod Sargon of Akkad? | He is not convinced, says the idea is old, and thinks the issue needs closer study. | He says the identification goes back to Sayce in the nineteenth century, not to later writers who repeat it, and he is not persuaded by the Sargon proposal. He has not settled who Nimrod is and says the question would require closer study, while criticizing scholarship that reuses older ideas without credit. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=3235" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:55</a> | Could the cemetery at Avaris have begun after the exodus because of deaths from the plagues? | It is possible and suggestive, but agricultural leveling makes it impossible to prove. | He says abandoned mudbrick houses at Avaris could have been converted into tombs by digging through the floors, so a plague-related cemetery is possible. The idea is suggestive because the city was turned into a cemetery instead of being repopulated, but agricultural leveling and disturbed remains prevent proving it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=3336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:36</a> | Does the Merneptah Stele saying Israel was laid waste hurt the biblical narrative? | No. He says the wording is poetic and the stele actually supports Israel being in the land as a people group alongside the Hurrians. | He quotes the line as saying Israel is laid waste and has no seed, then treats it as poetic and political language rather than evidence that Israel vanished. Because Israel is named as a people group rather than a place and appears alongside another tribal group, he thinks the stele supports the biblical picture of Israel already being in the land with groups such as the Hurrians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=3641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:41</a> | Could the pillar of cloud and fire have a natural explanation? | He says historians cannot confirm or deny miracles, but Exodus often includes natural components, so natural weather and divine action need not be mutually exclusive. | He says historians cannot adjudicate miracles, but Exodus often pairs divine action with natural features, such as wind at the Reed Sea. He suggests the pillar may involve unusual meteorological phenomena like storms, dust, or fire, and argues that reception history, natural events, and God working through nature are not mutually exclusive categories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=3980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:20</a> | If Amraphel in Genesis 14 is Hammurabi, why has that view been abandoned? | He says the abandonment is often ideological or chronology-driven, and he still finds the Hammurabi connection etymologically plausible. | He says liberal scholars resist the identification because it would give Abraham a historical synchronism, while some conservative chronologies resist it because it disrupts preferred dates. He still finds Hammurabi a better etymological fit than alternatives such as Amarsin, especially because of how the initial consonant could shift when borrowed through Akkadian and other Semitic languages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=4252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:52</a> | Which Egyptian creation myths would Israelites have encountered, and where can one read more? | He says they would have been exposed broadly to Egyptian and other regional creation traditions, and suggests general works on Egyptian gods and mythology. | He says Israelites in Avaris would have encountered a broad mix of Egyptian, Canaanite, Libyan, Nubian, Kushite, and Levantine ideas because the city was cosmopolitan. For reading, he does not name a single focused source but points to publications on Egyptian and Ugaritic creation myths and general works such as books on Egyptian religion and gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=4433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:53</a> | Did Moses write Genesis 1-11, or was it a later Babylonian exile addition? | He rejects a Babylonian exile addition and thinks Genesis was written during the Sinai period, possibly by scribes under Moses' authority. | He says Genesis was written during the Sinai period, though he leaves open whether Moses wrote it personally or whether scribes and amanuenses wrote under his authority, as with later examples such as Jeremiah and Baruch. He rejects a Babylonian exile addition because he thinks the etymology in Genesis does not fit that later setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=4535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:35</a> | Where can artists find accurate information for ancient-world clothing, tools, and objects? | He recommends archaeological reports, museum collections, some consultant-backed visual books, Petrie on tools, and Geoffrey Killen on furniture. | He recommends starting with archaeological reports and museum collections because they show actual tools, furniture, and construction details. He also notes that some visual books for younger readers use archaeological consultants, and names Petrie for tools and Geoffrey Killen for furniture as useful picture-rich sources. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=4740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:00</a> | Was reincarnation ever part of Gnostic belief? | No. He says Gnosticism saw flesh negatively, so reincarnation would be contrary to its aim of liberation from the body. | He says reincarnation would be anathema to Gnosticism because Gnostics viewed flesh as evil and spirit as good. Since their goal was liberation of the soul from the body, returning to another body would be the opposite of salvation in that system. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=4809" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:09</a> | Is there evidence that Midian was in the Sinai Peninsula besides Moses going west? | Direct evidence is scarce, but biblical and ecological evidence make Midianite pastoral presence in Sinai plausible. | He says Midianite pastoralists ranged broadly across Arabia, Edom, and into Sinai, but direct evidence for mobile groups is hard to pin down. The stronger case is consistency: biblical texts place Midianites in Sinai, and ecological studies indicate Sinai had savannah conditions that could support pastoral movement in the relevant periods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=4947" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:27</a> | Why did God want to kill Moses in the bridegroom of blood incident? | Moses had failed to circumcise his children and was therefore neglecting the Abrahamic covenant he was about to represent. | He says Moses was being sent to represent the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, yet had failed to apply the covenant sign of circumcision to his own children. Zipporah's action averts the crisis, and the apparently abrupt narrative is meant to be read against the Abrahamic covenant rather than as a disconnected episode. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=5180" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:20</a> | Is there Egyptian mythology about giants that could contextualize biblical giants in Canaan? | Not really. Egyptian art can portray gods or kings as oversized, but he does not see a myth of human giants. | He says Egyptian material can portray gods, kings, or important figures as physically oversized to express status and power. That artistic convention does not amount to a mythological tradition about human giants comparable to biblical giants in Canaan. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=5255" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:35</a> | Who ruled Egypt when the king was away on campaign? | The vizier handled day-to-day administration and would run the kingdom when the king was away. | He says rule reverted administratively to the vizier, with one vizier before the New Kingdom and two when the kingdom became larger. The vizier already managed most day-to-day government and important decisions that did not rise to the king, so the kingdom could function while the king was away. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=5372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:32</a> | Were the ten plagues judgments on ten specific Egyptian gods? | No. He says the text specifically frames the tenth plague as judgment on Egypt's gods and power structure, not each plague as targeting one god. | He rejects the one-plague-per-god theory because the biblical text only explicitly links judgment on Egypt's gods with the death of the firstborn. He interprets that plague as an attack on Egypt's power structure, since firstborn heirs, priests, officials, military leaders, and royal succession were intertwined with Egyptian religion and politics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=5658" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:18</a> | Were there cultural differences between Upper and Lower Egypt? | Some, especially diet and environment, but preservation in Lower Egypt is difficult and the differences were not huge. | He says Lower Egypt is hard to assess because the wet environment preserves poorly, but diet likely differed because the delta had more fish, fowl, game, and hunting while Upper Egypt leaned more heavily on wheat agriculture. By the New Kingdom some differences had narrowed, and both regions still practiced inundation agriculture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=5805" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:45</a> | Could Moses have been addressing the grand vizier rather than Pharaoh? | He rejects this; the vizier was not called Pharaoh and lacked the authority shown in the exodus narrative. | He rejects the proposal partly because it assumes a Thutmose III-era exodus he does not accept, but more directly because `Pharaoh` functioned as the king's title by that period. A vizier could act for the king in administration, but would not be called Pharaoh and would not have the authority shown in the exodus narrative, such as imposing or escalating national labor policy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6w6NTLN3two?t=6130" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:10</a> | Have archaeologists found the land of Goshen in Egypt? | Yes in the sense that Goshen is the region around Avaris and Pi-Ramesses, whose locations are known. | He explains Goshen as a region rather than a single city, tied to the area around Avaris, Tell el-Dab'a, Qantir, and Pi-Ramesses. Since those landmarks have been identified and the surrounding land is still notably fertile, he says archaeologists effectively know where Goshen was. |
